directions.research_question: Does harness-updating flatness generalize across different evolution algorithms, or is it an artifact of the simple adaptive_skill algorithm?
directions.hypothesis: Flatness breaks under RL-based or search-based evolution algorithms that amplify evolver capability differences.
directions.minimum_viable_experiment: Repeat evolver-side analysis (3 evolvers × 3 anchors) on SWE-bench using 3 different evolution algorithms: adaptive_skill (current), SkillRL-style (RL-based skill expansion), GEPA-style (prompt-level reflection). Measure Δ_update for each algorithm.
directions.negative_result_interpretation: If flatness persists across all 3 algorithms → harness-updating is genuinely a low-ceiling task where model capability doesn't matter. If flatness breaks under RL/search → current findings are algorithm-dependent.
directions.killer_objection: Algorithm-model interaction confounds: a different algorithm may favor different model families, producing sorting that reflects compatibility rather than capability.
directions.killer_result: One algorithm shows a 15+ pp spread across evolvers where another shows <3 pp — clear evidence of algorithm dependency.
directions.first_week_plan: 1. Identify 2 compatible evolution algorithms from the literature. 2. Implement or adapt their prompt interfaces. 3. Run 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 experimental cells (3 evolvers × 3 anchors × 3 algorithms). 4. Analyze Δ_update variance components.

directions.research_question: Are activation and adherence causally separable, or is one a proxy for the other?
directions.hypothesis: Activation and adherence are causally distinct skills that improve via different training interventions.
directions.minimum_viable_experiment: Construct adversarial harnesses: (a) activation test: harness that requires non-standard format loading; (b) adherence test: harness with contingent branching logic. Test 6 models on these adversarial harnesses, measure SLR and HFR. Check whether SLR and HFR correlate across models.
directions.negative_result_interpretation: If SLR and HFR perfectly correlate → they may be two expressions of a single underlying capability (general instruction following). If they dissociate → paper's claim of two distinct modes is confirmed.
directions.killer_objection: The adversarial harnesses themselves may confound task difficulty with skill measurement.
directions.killer_result: A model with high SLR but low HFR on the same adversarial harness → causal separation confirmed.
directions.first_week_plan: 1. Design 5 activation-adversarial harnesses. 2. Design 5 adherence-adversarial harnesses. 3. Set up SkillsBench evaluation pipeline. 4. Run 6 models through both sets. 5. Compute SLR/HFR correlation matrix.

directions.research_question: Can targeted training on long-horizon instruction following close the weak-tier adherence gap?
directions.hypothesis: Weak-model adherence decay is trainable: a supervised approach that injects periodic 're-grounding' prompts into training trajectories can reduce drift by 50%.
directions.minimum_viable_experiment: Take Qwen3-32B. Construct a training dataset of SkillsBench-like tasks with explicit re-grounding signals at trajectory midpoints. Fine-tune with LoRA. Compare adherence drift (load → final) against base model. Control: fine-tune on same tasks without re-grounding signals.
directions.negative_result_interpretation: If adherence drift is unchanged after training → the bottleneck is in the model's architecture or pretraining, not in fine-tuning data. If drift decreases but Δ_benefit doesn't improve → adherence may not be the binding constraint.
directions.killer_objection: Fine-tuning for adherence on one benchmark may not transfer to other benchmarks — the training intervention may be task-specific rather than capability-general.
directions.killer_result: Adherence drift drops from -0.39 to -0.15 with Δ_benefit improving 2× on SWE → intervention is effective and general.
directions.first_week_plan: 1. Construct re-grounding training data from 20 SkillsBench tasks. 2. Set up LoRA fine-tuning pipeline for Qwen3-32B. 3. Train with and without re-grounding. 4. Evaluate on held-out SkillsBench tasks. 5. Report adherence drift and Δ_benefit.

directions.research_question: How much of the harness-updating gain is attributable to content vs. mere presence of structured text?
directions.hypothesis: Random procedural text injected as 'skills' produces similar gains to genuine evolved skills, suggesting the content itself is not the active ingredient.
directions.minimum_viable_experiment: Create a 'sham evolver' condition: for each task, inject a procedurally-formatted but semantically empty skill (pseudorandom instructions with matched length/format). Compare Δ_update against real evolver conditions on SkillsBench.
directions.negative_result_interpretation: If sham skills produce 0-1pp gain vs 2-4pp for real evolvers → content matters. If sham skills produce 2-4pp gain → the act of injection (not the content) is the main driver, fundamentally weakening the flatness finding.
directions.killer_objection: The sham skill needs to be carefully controlled to avoid accidentally being useful or harmful — length, format, and action type must be matched.
directions.killer_result: Sham skills produce Δ_update ≥ 70% of real skill Δ_update → content contribution is secondary to structure.
directions.first_week_plan: 1. Write a sham skill generator that produces format-matched but content-randomized procedural text. 2. Run 3 evolver conditions (none, sham, Qwen3.5-9B) × 3 anchors on SkillsBench. 3. Compare Δ_update. 4. Also test content-free vs content-full with a held-out judge.
